-- DECISIONS (201 total, 3 core + last 30) ---------------------
<decision id="gad-04">
  <title>All session intents, decisions, and guidance must be captured in planning docs each session</title>
  <summary>Every working session that produces new decisions, requirements, or guidance must update DECISIONS.xml and the relevant planning docs before the session ends. Chat context is ephemeral â€” planning docs are the durable record. The GAD planning loop is the mechanism; this decision makes the capture requirement explicit.</summary>
  <impact>Agents starting a new session get current decisions from the planning docs, not from reconstructing chat history. Drift between chat intent and planning state is prevented.</impact>
</decision>
<decision id="gad-17">
  <title>Context exhaustion: work through auto-compact by default, suggest restart at clean boundaries when economically motivated</title>
  <summary>**Default behavior:** never stop work mid-task, never ask the user to restart, work through Claude Code's auto-compact event. After compaction, run `gad snapshot --projectid <id>` to re-hydrate and continue. The planning docs (STATE.xml next-action, TASK-REGISTRY.xml, DECISIONS.xml) are what make this possible â€” they are the durable state, not the conversation history.

**Refinement (2026-04-13):** The original "never suggest restart" rule is too absolute. There are real costs to letting a session grow indefinitely, and there are clean moments where a restart is the right call. The agent should *loosely* suggest restarts when the economics favor it.

**Cost shape of a long session:**
1. Every turn re-sends the entire conversation history as input tokens. Turn 100 pays for turns 1-99 again. Cost per turn grows linearly with session length.
2. Anthropic prompt cache has a 5-minute TTL. Idle gaps cold-start the cache and force a full re-read at full price.
3. As context fills, the model attends to more irrelevant history. Quality on the *current* task degrades even though the old turns are technically still there.
4. Context window is finite. Hitting the wall mid-task forces an unplanned auto-compact that is lossier than a planned restart.

**When a restart suggestion is appropriate:**
- A phase or large task just landed and its decisions are now captured in planning docs (rehydration story is good)
- The next chunk of work is clearly scoped and self-contained (no dangling in-head state)
- Topic is switching (e.g., "we're done with phase 43, now let's look at phase 44 marketplace design")
- The agent's context is past ~60-70% and the next task is independent
- Auto-compact is imminent â€” a planned restart now beats a forced compaction mid-task

**When a restart is NOT appropriate:**
- Mid-task with unwritten state in the agent's head
- During an investigation where the trail of "what we ruled out" carries weight
- When the next thing depends on subtle context from the last few turns
- Any time the user is in flow and a restart would interrupt their thinking

**How to suggest:** loosely, with one-line reasoning ("phase done, context ~70%, next chunk is mechanical and rehydratable from snapshot â€” restart is reasonable here, or I can keep going"). The user decides. Never push, never restart unilaterally. The default still leans toward continuing â€” restart is the exception, not the norm.</summary>
  <impact>Agents work through auto-compact by default and never restart unilaterally. They MAY suggest a restart loosely at clean planning boundaries when context is filling and the next chunk has a good rehydration story via `gad snapshot`. The user always decides. gad-04 (decisions in planning docs) is still what makes both auto-compact and planned restarts work â€” without durable docs, no rehydration story exists at all.</impact>
</decision>
<decision id="gad-18">
  <title>The GAD loop is simple: snapshot â†’ work â†’ update docs â†’ commit</title>
  <summary>The canonical GAD loop for any agent, any coding tool:
1. `gad snapshot --projectid <id>` â€” one command, full context. No reading 15 files manually.
2. Pick one task from TASK-REGISTRY.xml (status=planned).
3. Implement it.
4. Update TASK-REGISTRY.xml (mark done), STATE.xml (next-action), DECISIONS.xml (if new decisions).
5. Commit.

That's it. AGENTS.md files in each project reference this loop but do NOT redefine it. The vendor/get-anything-done/ project is the canonical source. Root AGENTS.md and .planning/AGENTS.md are consumers that point here, not parallel definitions.

Skills are methodology documents (SKILL.md) â€” agents read and follow them. They are not CLI commands or executable plugins. The GAD CLI provides context efficiency. Skills provide methodology.</summary>
  <impact>Root AGENTS.md mandatory read order must be simplified. Agents should NOT read 15 files on startup â€” gad snapshot exists for this. AGENTS.md files across vendor projects reference the GAD loop, they don't each define their own version. No memory files, no shadow task lists â€” TASK-REGISTRY.xml and STATE.xml are the only task tracking.</impact>
</decision>

  framework-upgrade
    Ship a change to the GAD framework itself (new skill, new subagent, reworked command, altered loop) in a way that preserves the ability to interpret eval result
  portfolio-sync
    Keep the public-facing GAD site and generated publishing artifacts in sync with the framework as it evolves. Trigger this skill whenever an eval run finishes, a

    Analyze GAD trace data (.gad-log JSONL + .trace-events.jsonl + preserved eval traces) to produce usage reports such as tool mix, skill invocations, commit rhyth

    Cut a new GAD release — version bump, CHANGELOG entry, Node SEA rebuild, git tag, and `gh release create` with artifacts attached. Operator-triggered only, no C

GAD session startup — one command gets you full context.

  gad snapshot --projectid <id>      # full context dump, ~6-7k tokens

What snapshot gives you:
  - STATE.xml (current phase, next-action, references)
  - ROADMAP in-sprint phases
  - Open + recently-done tasks in sprint window
  - Recent decisions (last 30)
  - EQUIPPED SKILLS (top 5 by relevance, with workflow pointers)
  - DOCS-MAP + file references

After reading snapshot output:
  1. Act on the <next-action> line in STATE.xml — this is what to do next.
  2. Browse EQUIPPED SKILLS for skills relevant to the current sprint.
     Use `gad skill show <id>` to inspect any skill end-to-end.
  3. Use `gad skill list --paths` for the full skill inventory with paths.

Cross-session continuity:
  - Decisions live in .planning/DECISIONS.xml — query with `gad decisions`.
  - Task attribution is on TASK-REGISTRY.xml entries (skill= attribute).
  - Re-run snapshot after auto-compact to re-hydrate.

Rehydration cost note (decision gad-195, 2026-04-15):
  Snapshot is ~6-7k tokens. Running it every turn wastes cache. Run it
  once at session start and at clean phase boundaries. Between those
  points, trust the planning doc edits you made — they are durable.

-- DECISIONS (201 total, 3 core + last 30) ---------------------
<decision id="gad-04">
  <title>All session intents, decisions, and guidance must be captured in planning docs each session</title>
  <summary>Every working session that produces new decisions, requirements, or guidance must update DECISIONS.xml and the relevant planning docs before the session ends. Chat context is ephemeral â€” planning docs are the durable record. The GAD planning loop is the mechanism; this decision makes the capture requirement explicit.</summary>
  <impact>Agents starting a new session get current decisions from the planning docs, not from reconstructing chat history. Drift between chat intent and planning state is prevented.</impact>
</decision>
<decision id="gad-17">
  <title>Context exhaustion: work through auto-compact by default, suggest restart at clean boundaries when economically motivated</title>
  <summary>**Default behavior:** never stop work mid-task, never ask the user to restart, work through Claude Code's auto-compact event. After compaction, run `gad snapshot --projectid <id>` to re-hydrate and continue. The planning docs (STATE.xml next-action, TASK-REGISTRY.xml, DECISIONS.xml) are what make this possible â€” they are the durable state, not the conversation history.

**Refinement (2026-04-13):** The original "never suggest restart" rule is too absolute. There are real costs to letting a session grow indefinitely, and there are clean moments where a restart is the right call. The agent should *loosely* suggest restarts when the economics favor it.

**Cost shape of a long session:**
1. Every turn re-sends the entire conversation history as input tokens. Turn 100 pays for turns 1-99 again. Cost per turn grows linearly with session length.
2. Anthropic prompt cache has a 5-minute TTL. Idle gaps cold-start the cache and force a full re-read at full price.
3. As context fills, the model attends to more irrelevant history. Quality on the *current* task degrades even though the old turns are technically still there.
4. Context window is finite. Hitting the wall mid-task forces an unplanned auto-compact that is lossier than a planned restart.

**When a restart suggestion is appropriate:**
- A phase or large task just landed and its decisions are now captured in planning docs (rehydration story is good)
- The next chunk of work is clearly scoped and self-contained (no dangling in-head state)
- Topic is switching (e.g., "we're done with phase 43, now let's look at phase 44 marketplace design")
- The agent's context is past ~60-70% and the next task is independent
- Auto-compact is imminent â€” a planned restart now beats a forced compaction mid-task

**When a restart is NOT appropriate:**
- Mid-task with unwritten state in the agent's head
- During an investigation where the trail of "what we ruled out" carries weight
- When the next thing depends on subtle context from the last few turns
- Any time the user is in flow and a restart would interrupt their thinking

**How to suggest:** loosely, with one-line reasoning ("phase done, context ~70%, next chunk is mechanical and rehydratable from snapshot â€” restart is reasonable here, or I can keep going"). The user decides. Never push, never restart unilaterally. The default still leans toward continuing â€” restart is the exception, not the norm.</summary>
  <impact>Agents work through auto-compact by default and never restart unilaterally. They MAY suggest a restart loosely at clean planning boundaries when context is filling and the next chunk has a good rehydration story via `gad snapshot`. The user always decides. gad-04 (decisions in planning docs) is still what makes both auto-compact and planned restarts work â€” without durable docs, no rehydration story exists at all.</impact>
</decision>
<decision id="gad-18">
  <title>The GAD loop is simple: snapshot â†’ work â†’ update docs â†’ commit</title>
  <summary>The canonical GAD loop for any agent, any coding tool:
1. `gad snapshot --projectid <id>` â€” one command, full context. No reading 15 files manually.
2. Pick one task from TASK-REGISTRY.xml (status=planned).
3. Implement it.
4. Update TASK-REGISTRY.xml (mark done), STATE.xml (next-action), DECISIONS.xml (if new decisions).
5. Commit.

That's it. AGENTS.md files in each project reference this loop but do NOT redefine it. The vendor/get-anything-done/ project is the canonical source. Root AGENTS.md and .planning/AGENTS.md are consumers that point here, not parallel definitions.

Skills are methodology documents (SKILL.md) â€” agents read and follow them. They are not CLI commands or executable plugins. The GAD CLI provides context efficiency. Skills provide methodology.</summary>
  <impact>Root AGENTS.md mandatory read order must be simplified. Agents should NOT read 15 files on startup â€” gad snapshot exists for this. AGENTS.md files across vendor projects reference the GAD loop, they don't each define their own version. No memory files, no shadow task lists â€” TASK-REGISTRY.xml and STATE.xml are the only task tracking.</impact>
</decision>
